name: Booster Pack
classification_notes: A bundle of 13 or more display-oriented typefaces spanning a wide range of styles including script, decorative, and expressive designs; categorised as display given the collection's character and the playful, graphic nature implied by the named fonts.
summary: A growing bundle of Taylor Penton's overflow and newer releases, collecting 13 typefaces at launch with seven more to be added through the year.
description: Sold as a companion to the Big Collection, the Booster Pack gathers typefaces that arrived late or outside the main release cycle, including collaborations and experiments. The 13 included fonts span a range of expressive display styles under names like Hell's Angles, Red Dye 40 and Stupid Cupid. Buyers receive future additions automatically as they release, and the licence covers commercial use for individuals and teams under 50 employees without seat or traffic restrictions.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes the Booster Pack as everything that didn't make it into the Big Collection: new work, collaborations and fonts that showed up late. Starting at 13 typefaces, it grows with seven further additions through the rest of the year, all covered under a single flat commercial licence with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Big Typeface Collection
classification_notes: The collection is described as 53 hand-drawn typefaces spanning a range of display styles; the hand-drawn character and decorative intent place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A bundle of 53 hand-drawn display typefaces from Taylor Penton, including nine exclusive fonts unavailable elsewhere in the store.
description: The collection brings together 53 hand-drawn typefaces under a single commercial licence, with nine fonts, including Woodrow, Nina and Cardano, exclusive to this bundle. Language support ranges from standard English to extensive Western European coverage depending on the individual typeface. Supplied as OTF desktop files and WOFF/WOFF2 webfonts, with a licence covering logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and commercial products for individuals and teams up to 50 employees.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes this as 53 hand-drawn typefaces wrapped up in a discounted bundle, noting that nine fonts, such as Woodrow, Nina and Cardano, are exclusive to this collection and cannot be purchased individually elsewhere in the store. It is a one-time purchase with a flat commercial licence and no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Closeout
classification_notes: The name, the playful product copy, and the companion slanted style all point to a display-oriented sans with a personality-led, vernacular character suited to branding and short-setting work.
summary: A two-style display family from Taylor Penton, comprising an upright and a slanted cut aimed at branding, packaging and identity work.
description: Closeout ships as two OTF files, Regular and Slanted, with 220 glyphs covering extensive Western European Latin including Icelandic and Welsh. The licensing is straightforward: a single flat-fee commercial licence for individuals and teams under 50 employees, covering logos, packaging, web, apps and broadcast with no seat counts or traffic caps. It sits alongside other Taylor Penton releases such as Benditos and Highbeams, suggesting a consistent focus on characterful display faces for small-studio and brand use.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton positions Closeout as a no-nonsense commercial release with a simple flat-fee licence covering logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale. The family includes Regular and Slanted cuts delivered as OTF, WOFF and WOFF2 files, with broad Western European language support including Icelandic and Welsh.

name: Hell's Angles
classification_notes: Described by its creators as ugly, miscalculated and odd, with contextual alternates in the lowercase and an intentionally chaotic, unrestrained design vocabulary that places it firmly in novelty display territory.
summary: A single-weight display face built from collaborative conflict, with contextual lowercase alternates that push its deliberately unruly character further.
description: Co-designed by Taylor Penton and a collaborator identified as Joe, the face wears its miscalculation as a feature, not a flaw. Contextual alternates are baked into the lowercase, shifting the texture of a word as you type. Coverage extends to Western European Latin including Icelandic and Welsh across 165 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describe Hell's Angles as what happens when two designers argue about direction for weeks and eventually abandon restraint entirely. The result is intentionally ugly and odd, co-built with Joe, with contextual alternates in the lowercase for added unpredictability. Licensed simply: one flat commercial fee covering individuals and teams under 50 employees, no seat counts, no web traffic caps.

name: Birdie
classification_notes: The page describes a 'handmade modern vibe' and the font is sold as a display typeface with contextual alternates for expressive use, placing it firmly in the display category with a handcrafted character.
summary: A single-weight display face with two full sets of contextual alternates across the entire upper and lowercase alphabet and digits, designed for a hand-drawn yet modern feel.
description: Sold in a 'Legendary Version' update, Birdie ships with two complete sets of contextual alternates for every letter and numeral, giving layouts natural variation without manually swapping glyphs. The 207-glyph character set covers extensive Western European languages including Icelandic and Welsh. Aimed at logos, packaging and branding work where a casual, approachable tone is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Birdie as a font with a handmade modern vibe, updated to its Legendary Version with two new sets of contextual alternates for the full alphabet and digits. It ships with a single flat-fee commercial licence covering logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale, with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Crumb
classification_notes: A display typeface with deliberate softness and imperfection evoking late-1990s graphic design sensibilities; the irregular, casual character places it firmly in the display/novelty category rather than any structured text classification.
summary: A single-weight display face with soft, slightly wonky letterforms drawn from late-1990s graphic nostalgia.
description: Built around deliberate imperfection, its rounded and gently irregular forms give it a handmade warmth suited to branding, packaging and editorial work with a casual or nostalgic tone. The 220-glyph set covers extensive Western European Latin including Icelandic and Welsh. Available as a single commercial licence with no seat or traffic restrictions for teams under 50 employees.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Crumb as a little soft, a little off, and a nod to the simpler aesthetic of the late 1990s. It comes with a flat-rate commercial licence covering logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale, with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Wingman
classification_notes: A hand-drawn sans-serif with an informal lean and bounce, inspired by 1950s poster lettering. Classified as display due to its expressive, illustrative character suited to headlines and branding rather than continuous text.
summary: A hand-drawn display sans inspired by 1950s poster lettering, with a slight forward lean and informal bounce suited to headlines and branding.
description: Part of Taylor Penton's bird-themed type family alongside Birdie and Big Bird, Wingman offers a single Regular weight with full upper and lowercase Latin. The hand-drawn construction gives it warmth and subtle irregularity at close range while reading as clean and restrained from a distance. Best used in headlines, packaging and brand work where an approachable retro feel is wanted without committing to full period pastiche.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Wingman as a hand-drawn sans with a friendly lean and a little bounce, inspired by 1950s poster lettering. The addition of lowercase means you no longer have to fake it. Aimed at headlines, branding and anything that needs to feel warm without going full retro.

name: Taxi Driver
classification_notes: A retro-inspired casual script with loose, connected letterforms designed to read as approachable yet polished, typical of brush-style casual scripts used in branding and packaging.
summary: A retro-styled casual script with loose letterforms pitched at logos, packaging and menu design that want a handcrafted feel without looking rough.
description: Described by its designer as the second iteration of the Taxi Driver family, this version adds threaded brows and a warmer character to the original. The single-weight OTF covers basic Latin with a limited set of accented characters, totalling 151 glyphs. At $25 with a broad commercial licence covering logos, packaging, websites and broadcast, it sits squarely in the affordable indie foundry space.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Taxi Driver as a retro-inspired script that stays loose and legible while being just refined enough to pass for upscale. It is positioned as casual enough for everyday use yet polished enough for client work, with particular suitability for logos, packaging and menus. The licence covers individuals, small businesses and teams under 50 employees with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Bone Quixote
classification_notes: A bold, single-weight display typeface with an irregular, hand-influenced edge drawn from a mid-20th century label reference; no italics or multiple weights place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A bold single-weight display face drawn from a mid-20th century label, with soft forms and a deliberately irregular edge.
description: Referencing a small piece of type found on a vintage label, the letterforms are heavy and rounded with a rough, organic quality that sets it apart from clean digital type. The R received particular attention from the designer, and the overall character suits packaging, logos and short headline work. Coverage is basic Latin with a limited set of accented characters across 151 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Bone Quixote as their newest font, originally referencing a small piece of type on a mid-20th century label. The characters are bold and soft with an irregular edge, and the R got a little extra attention. It ships with a single flat-fee commercial licence covering logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale, with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Mini Collection
classification_notes: A bundle of 14 distinct display typefaces spanning a wide range of styles; no single category applies, though the collection skews toward decorative and display use cases typical of independent type designers working in branding and packaging.
summary: A bundle of 14 display typefaces by Taylor Penton, sold together as a lower-cost entry point into the full collection.
description: Fourteen named typefaces are packaged here at a reduced price, covering a broad stylistic range suited to branding, packaging and editorial work. Each font ships as OTF for desktop and WOFF/WOFF2 for web, under a single commercial licence with no seat or traffic restrictions for teams under 50. Language support varies by typeface but spans Western European scripts including Icelandic and Welsh.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes the Mini Collection as a bite-sized bundle of 14 typefaces made with love, offering a straightforward commercial licence with no seat counts or web traffic caps, covering logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale.

name: Flapjack
classification_notes: A hand-drawn display face modelled on 1920s billboard and shop sign lettering, placing it firmly in the display category with a vintage vernacular subcategory.
summary: A hand-lettered display face drawn from 1920s billboard and shop sign lettering, suited to logos, packaging and vintage-styled branding.
description: Each letter was drawn by hand, referencing the informal lettering found on old shop signs and painted billboards from the 1920s. The single Regular weight ships as OTF, WOFF and WOFF2, with basic Latin coverage and a limited set of accented characters across 149 glyphs. It suits work that calls for nostalgic warmth without the rigidity of a revived period typeface.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describe Flapjack as a collaborative creation with Joe, hand-drawn to evoke the look of 1920s billboards and shop signs. They position it as equally at home on large signage or small menus, and include a flat commercial licence covering individuals and teams of up to 50 employees.

name: Bread Pan
classification_notes: Inspired by the angular, utilitarian forms of bread clips, Bread Pan sits firmly in the display category as a novelty hand-drawn face with a quirky, informal character unsuited to running text.
summary: A single-weight display face drawn from the angular forms of bread clips, sitting somewhere between hand-lettered and industrial oddity.
description: The letterforms take their cues from the small plastic tags used to seal bread bags, resulting in a simple, slightly irregular face with an unpretentious charm. Sold as a single Regular weight with OTF and webfont files, it covers Western European Latin with accented characters for Icelandic, Welsh and Central European languages. Best suited to packaging, branding and short-copy work where a low-key, handmade feel is wanted without heavy vintage affectation.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Bread Pan as the product of years studying the typographic forms of bread clips, resulting in a font that is simple, unassuming and just the right amount of weird, intended for designs that want to feel fresh.

name: Highbeams
classification_notes: Described by the designer as a jittery hand-type, placing it firmly in the display category with hand-lettered characteristics rather than any formal type classification.
summary: A jittery hand-lettered display face with regular, italic and small-caps cuts, carrying the rough energy of hand-drawn lettering.
description: Designed by Taylor Penton, Highbeams leans into the uneven quality of hand-drawn type, citing the influence of letterer Jon Contino as a loose touchstone. The family ships in two styles, Regular and Italic, both as OTF with webfont formats included, and covers extensive Western European Latin with small-caps built in. At £25 with a single-licence model covering logos, packaging and broadcast, it sits in the indie display market for branding and editorial work that wants a hand-made feel.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Highbeams as a font that was supposed to be clean and polished but ended up as a jittery hand-type with regular, italic and small-caps baked in, citing the ghost of Jon Contino filtered through poor choices. It ships with OTF and webfont files and a straightforward flat commercial licence covering individuals and teams under 50 employees.

name: Cupid
classification_notes: Described explicitly as semi-monospaced with handwriting origins, placing it in the mono category with a casual, script-influenced character rather than a technical or coding context.
summary: A semi-monospaced typeface drawn from handwriting sources, made in collaboration with Joe Goldsmith and aimed at casual branding and personal use.
description: Developed with Joe Goldsmith, Cupid takes handwritten letterforms and tidies them into a semi-monospaced structure suitable for logos, packaging and wedding stationery. The single Regular weight ships as OTF, WOFF and WOFF2 with 150 glyphs covering basic Latin and a limited set of accented characters. Reviewers have used it for wedding collateral and brand identity work where an informal but legible hand is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Cupid as a semi-monospaced typeface made in collaboration with Mr. Joe Goldsmith, likening it to taking a sister's handwriting and polishing it into something more refined. It comes with a straightforward single commercial licence covering individuals, small businesses and teams under 50 employees, with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Flight Bundle
classification_notes: The bundle groups three display typefaces (Birdie, Big Bird, Wingman) under a single purchase; without individual specimen detail, display is the most fitting umbrella category given the foundry's decorative, expressive output.
summary: A bundle of three display typefaces from Taylor Penton sold together at a reduced price with a simple flat commercial licence.
description: Birdie, Big Bird and Wingman are packaged here as a single purchase for those who want the full set rather than individual fonts. Each typeface carries its own character whilst sitting within Penton's broader expressive, display-oriented output. The flat licence covers logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps and broadcast for individuals and teams under 50 employees, with no seat counting or web traffic caps.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton frames the Flight Bundle as the font equivalent of a beer or coffee flight: a way to get Birdie, Big Bird and Wingman together in one go. The bundle ships with OTF desktop files and WOFF/WOFF2 webfonts, covered by a single commercial licence for individuals, small businesses and teams under 50 employees across a wide range of use cases.

name: Type Volume One
classification_notes: A bundled collection of twelve distinct display and decorative typefaces spanning multiple styles; no single category applies, so display is used as the broadest accurate descriptor.
summary: A bundle of twelve display typefaces from Taylor Penton, sold together for commercial use across branding, packaging and web work.
description: Twelve named display fonts sold as a single commercial bundle at a flat price, covering a range of expressive styles suited to logos, packaging and client work. The licence is straightforward: one flat fee for individuals and teams under fifty employees, with no seat counts or web traffic caps, and includes OTF desktop files alongside WOFF and WOFF2 webfonts. Language support covers Western European scripts including Icelandic and Welsh, though coverage varies by individual font.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Volume One as twelve fonts with no filler and no speculative picks, positioned as a practical alternative to late-night free font browsing. The flat commercial licence covers logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale, with an extended licence available for teams of fifty or more.

name: Type Volume Two
classification_notes: A bundled collection of twelve typefaces spanning multiple styles and categories, sold as a single product; classified as display due to the decorative and expressive nature of the included fonts and their intended use in branding, marketing and social media.
summary: A bundle of twelve display-leaning typefaces from Taylor Penton, aimed at branding, packaging and social media work.
description: Twelve typefaces sold together at a single flat price, covering a range of expressive and display styles suited to brand identity, marketing materials and packaging. The collection includes Waffle Cone, Bone Quixote, Milkbar, Rosemary, Wawa, Dumpling, Lennon, Lulu!, Amaya, Losa, Danny Lasso and Brunch, with OTF desktop files and WOFF/WOFF2 webfonts included. A flat commercial licence covers individuals and teams under 50 employees with no seat or traffic limits.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Volume 2 as twelve typefaces that work across marketing material, branding and social posts, pitched as a single pack that makes any project look considered. The licence is kept simple with no seat counts or web traffic caps, covering logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale.

name: Type Volume Three
classification_notes: A bundle of twelve distinct typefaces spanning a range of display styles including scripts, decorative, and expressive faces; no single classification applies, but display best captures the collection's retail positioning.
summary: A bundle of twelve display typefaces from independent designer Taylor Penton, sold under a single flat-fee commercial licence.
description: Twelve named typefaces sold together as a mid-range bundle, covering a range of expressive and display styles from Basal to Tommy Tokyo. The licence is deliberately straightforward: no seat counts or traffic caps, covering logos, packaging, websites, apps and broadcast for individuals and teams under fifty. Western European language support is included across all faces, with depth varying by individual typeface.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Volume 3 as the middle child of the series, not trying to impress anyone but delivering strong results on its own terms. The bundle includes twelve typefaces, several of which are exclusive to this volume, with OTF desktop files and WOFF/WOFF2 webfonts included alongside a standard commercial EULA.

name: Type Volume Four
classification_notes: A bundle of twelve hand-drawn display typefaces with mid-century character, spanning retro and modern directions. The hand-lettered, illustrative nature places them firmly in the display/script-adjacent category.
summary: A collection of twelve hand-drawn display typefaces with a mid-century character, ranging from retro to modern in feel.
description: Twelve individually named hand-lettered faces sold as a single bundle, each with its own personality but united by a hand-drawn warmth and mid-century sensibility. Some lean toward vintage Americana or diner signage; others sit closer to contemporary casual lettering. The bundle is aimed at branding, packaging and web use, covered under a single flat-fee commercial licence with no seat or traffic restrictions.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Volume Four as structured with a mid-century feel, noting that while some faces lean modern and others lean retro, each one is unique and hand-drawn. The bundle includes twelve typefaces and ships with OTF desktop files and WOFF/WOFF2 webfonts, with Western European language support including Icelandic and Welsh.

name: Dumpling
classification_notes: A single-weight bold display face with rounded, smooth letterforms described as bold and smooth; fits display sans rather than a functional text category given its single weight and expressive character.
summary: A single-weight bold display face with smooth, rounded letterforms suited to branding, packaging and logo work.
description: Offered in one weight only, Dumpling leans on chunky, organic forms that give it character without becoming chaotic. The 149-glyph set covers basic Latin with a limited selection of accented characters, keeping scope modest but usable for Western European languages. At £25 with a flat commercial licence covering logos, web, apps and broadcast, it sits in a practical mid-market tier for independent designers.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Dumpling as bold, smooth and weirdly versatile, noting that the letterforms are strong enough to hold a design together even when other choices are questionable. It comes as a single Regular weight with OTF and webfont files, and an optional illustration pack add-on. Licensing is a flat commercial licence for individuals and teams under 50 employees, covering logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale.

name: Danny Lasso
classification_notes: A display typeface with handmade character and distinctive crossbar styling, positioned as an expressive spin-off with alternates; fits display or script-adjacent display category given its decorative, personality-driven nature.
summary: A single-weight display face spun off from Johnny Jukebox, distinguished by unique crossbar styling and regular alternates for added versatility.
description: Built on the structural bones of Johnny Jukebox, Danny Lasso takes its own direction through distinctive crossbar letterforms and a set of alternates that add variety without losing cohesion. The 150-glyph set covers basic Latin with a limited range of accented characters, making it best suited to logotypes, packaging and short display copy. A flat $25 commercial licence covers most use cases without seat or traffic restrictions.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Danny Lasso as a spin-off with its own personality, inspired by the structure of Johnny Jukebox. It introduces unique crossbar styling alongside regular alternates to keep things versatile, pitched for situations where you want something that stands out but still plays nice.

name: Dreamboat
classification_notes: Wide forms, soft edges and intentionally unrefined detail position this firmly as a display typeface; the lack of italic, limited glyph set and two-weight range reinforce its decorative, headline-focused purpose.
summary: A wide, soft-edged display sans in two weights, designed for headlines and branding work where personality matters more than neutrality.
description: Wide letterforms and softened edges give it an approachable, slightly casual character without tipping into novelty territory. Two weights, Thin and Regular, cover the essentials for display use, and the 151-glyph set handles basic Latin with a limited spread of accented characters. Suits packaging, logos and short headline copy where a warm, easygoing tone is the point.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Dreamboat as a slightly-too-attractive display typeface with wide forms, soft edges and just enough unrefined detail to keep it interesting, available in Thin and Regular weights with a single flat-fee commercial licence covering individuals, small businesses and teams under 50 employees.

name: Second String
classification_notes: The foundry describes clean forms and subtle nostalgia, suggesting a geometric or near-geometric sans with vintage inflection suited to branding and packaging.
summary: A single-weight sans with clean geometry and a hint of nostalgia, aimed at branding and packaging work.
description: Released as a single Regular weight, Second String keeps its glyph set lean at 151 characters with basic Latin and a limited range of accented forms. The foundry positions it for branding, packaging and identity work where a quietly retro quality is wanted without overt period styling. At £25 with a flat commercial licence covering logos, web, print and broadcast, it sits at the accessible end of the independent type market.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Second String as a typeface with clean forms, subtle nostalgia and broad versatility across branding, packaging and anything that benefits from feeling quietly timeless. It ships as a single commercial licence with no seat counts or web traffic caps, covering individuals and teams under 50 employees.

name: Sundae
classification_notes: A vintage-inspired hand-lettered script in the sign painter tradition, with upright or slightly slanted letterforms typical of painted display lettering rather than formal calligraphic scripts.
summary: A vintage sign painter script with strong display personality, sold as a single commercial weight at a flat rate.
description: Designed in the sign painter tradition, it carries the irregularity and warmth of hand-lettered display work without leaning too far into formal calligraphy. The single Regular weight ships as OTF, WOFF and WOFF2 with 205 glyphs covering Western European Latin including Icelandic and Welsh. The flat-fee licensing covers logos, packaging, websites and broadcast for individuals and small teams without seat or traffic restrictions.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Sundae as a vintage-inspired sign painter script with enough personality to carry a layout when the underlying idea falls short, noting it was not especially enjoyable to make but may well have been worth it. A single commercial licence covers most commercial uses for teams under 50 employees, with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Makawao
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a slightly tropical display font with a smooth, relaxed character, placing it firmly in casual display territory rather than any formal serif or sans-serif classification.
summary: A single-weight casual display face with a warm, unhurried character suited to branding, packaging and logotype work.
description: Built around a relaxed, slightly tropical sensibility, Makawao is offered in one weight with basic Latin coverage and a limited set of accented characters across 151 glyphs. The commercial licence covers logos, packaging, websites and broadcast without seat or traffic restrictions, making it straightforward for small studios and independent designers. Pairs well with other faces in the Taylor Penton catalogue such as Napili and Wawa.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describe Makawao as a slightly tropical display font with nothing to prove — smooth, relaxed, and suited to work that should feel warm, easy and a little less corporate. The licence is designed to be simple: one flat fee for individuals, small businesses and teams under 50 employees, covering commercial use across most common applications.

name: Fortune
classification_notes: Described as chunky with strong legs, Fortune reads as a bold display serif with personality, suited to headlines and branding rather than text setting.
summary: A single-weight chunky display serif with a retro character, built for headlines and logo work with a 70s desert aesthetic.
description: A bold, heavy display serif with pronounced strokes and a distinctive personality suited to logos, packaging and short-form display copy. The glyph set is modest at 151 characters, covering basic Latin with a limited selection of accented forms. Customer responses point toward vintage and handcrafted applications, particularly branding work with a retro or analogue quality.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describe Fortune as a chunky typeface with strong legs and a strong opinion, designed to stand out, slow things down and add weight to a design. They recommend it for settings where type needs to make a statement, supplied as a single Regular weight with OTF and webfont files under a flat commercial licence covering individuals and teams under 50 employees.

name: Jukebox Johnny
classification_notes: A wide-set, single-weight display sans with soft curves and vintage character, suited to headlines and packaging rather than continuous text. Its retro inflection and limited weight range place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A wide-set display sans with soft curves and vintage character, sold as a single commercial licence for $25.
description: Wide proportions and gentle curves give it a laid-back, mid-century quality without leaning into pastiche. Available in one weight as OTF, WOFF and WOFF2 files, with basic Latin coverage and a limited set of accented characters across 151 glyphs. Positioned for headlines, packaging and branding work where a relaxed retro feel is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Jukebox Johnny as carrying vintage energy without being stuck in the past — a wide-set sans with soft curves and balanced structure, designed for headlines, packaging, or anything needing quiet confidence. The licence covers logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale, with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Slowpoke
classification_notes: The face is explicitly described as a pseudo-architectural handwriting typeface, blending personal scribble with the drafting-style lettering common to architectural practice. This places it firmly in the display/script-adjacent space rather than a conventional script or sans category.
summary: A single-weight architectural handwriting face with contextual alternates that cycle through A-Z and 0-9 glyph variations to replicate natural hand-lettered irregularity.
description: Drawn from the overlap between architectural drafting lettering and the designer's own scribbled notes, the face carries an informal, human quality well suited to branding, presentation graphics and printed collateral. Contextual alternates automatically rotate through alternate forms for each letter and numeral, reducing the repetition that typically undermines single-style handwriting fonts. Western European language support is broad, covering Icelandic, Welsh and other extended Latin characters across 220 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Slowpoke as a pseudo-architectural handwriting typeface combining influences from his architect father and his own note-scribbling habits. The standout feature is a full set of A-Z and 0-9 contextual alternates that cycle automatically, lending the typeface what Penton calls a pathway to many abilities that some consider to be natural. Supplied as OTF desktop and WOFF/WOFF2 webfonts under a single flat-rate commercial licence covering individuals and teams of up to 50 employees.

name: Kula
classification_notes: Kula is described as hand-drawn with a slightly imperfect, natural feel, featuring caps and small caps with expressive character. This places it firmly in the display category as a hand-drawn or informal display face.
summary: A single-weight hand-drawn display face offering caps and small caps with a deliberately imperfect, informal character.
description: Kula comes in one weight and provides two full glyph sets, uppercase and small caps, both carrying a slightly irregular, handcrafted quality. The face sits at a relaxed, informal register without tipping into chaos, making it suited to branding, packaging and editorial work that wants warmth over polish. Glyph coverage is basic Latin with a limited selection of accented characters, totalling 151 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Kula as laid-back, slightly weird, and easy to get along with. It offers two full sets of caps and small caps with a slightly imperfect, natural feel, expressive without getting chaotic, and suited to any context that could use some personality. The licence covers commercial use for individuals and teams under 50 employees, with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Lennon
classification_notes: A single-weight display face drawn from vintage packaging lettering traditions, with rounded terminals and wide letterforms that place it firmly in the retro/display category rather than any functional text classification.
summary: A single-weight display face drawn from vintage packaging lettering, with rounded terminals, wide letterforms and deliberate hand-drawn irregularities.
description: Intended for packaging, logos and short-burst branding work, Lennon keeps its charm restrained enough to stay legible at a range of sizes. The rounded terminals and slightly uneven stroke quality recall mid-century product lettering without tipping into pastiche. Western European language support, including Icelandic and Welsh, was expanded in a 2026 update alongside a fuller punctuation set.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Lennon as a present-day take on vintage packaging lettering, with rounded edges, wide letterforms and hand-drawn imperfections that give it charm without getting too cute. They note it is one of their most popular fonts, citing its ease of use, and the family received updated language support and punctuation in February 2026.

name: Lana
classification_notes: Marketed as a single-weight expressive face inspired by spicy food, with the designer encouraging stretching and squashing for effect; clearly a display typeface with a playful, editorial character rather than a neutral text face.
summary: A single-weight expressive display face from Taylor Penton, conceived around the energy of spicy food and intended for stretching and squashing in branding contexts.
description: A one-weight display face with a lively, distorted character suited to food branding, packaging and logo work. The designer explicitly encourages warping proportions to match the visual heat of the specimen photography, making it more of a raw ingredient than a finished system. Basic Latin coverage with a limited set of accented characters keeps the glyph count at 150.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Lana as inspired by one thing only: spicy food. They encourage users to break the rules and stretch or squash the letterforms to match the energy of the featured photography. It ships as a single Regular weight in OTF and webfont formats under a flat commercial licence covering individuals, small businesses and teams under 50 employees.

name: Losa
classification_notes: Self-described as a display font with organic forms and steady structure, placing it squarely in the display category with an informal, hand-influenced character rather than any classical serif or sans-serif tradition.
summary: A single-weight organic display face with an easy, unpretentious character suited to branding and packaging work.
description: Losa comes in one weight and leans on softly organic letterforms to sit comfortably at large sizes without feeling showy. The glyph set covers 150 characters including basic Latin and a working set of Western European accents. At $25 with a flat commercial licence covering logos, packaging and web use, it is aimed squarely at independent designers and small studios.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Losa as a versatile display font that feels easy and honest, organic in form and steady in structure, designed to look good without demanding attention. It ships as a single Regular weight with desktop OTF and webfont files, and is sold under a straightforward flat commercial licence with no seat counts or web traffic caps for individuals and teams under 50 employees.

name: Milkbar
classification_notes: Described by the designer as a 'fat ass sans', Milkbar is a heavy, expressive display sans intended for impact rather than extended text use, fitting the display category.
summary: A single-weight, heavy display sans with a playful, chunky character aimed at branding, packaging and editorial work.
description: Released by Taylor Penton, Milkbar is a bold, wide display sans built for headlines and logo work where personality is the point. It ships as a single Regular weight with 151 glyphs covering Western European Latin, including Icelandic and Welsh. A late 2025 update extended the character set with broader language support and additional punctuation.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton pitches Milkbar as the 'fat ass sans font we never knew we needed', designed to bring life to design work. It comes with a single commercial licence covering logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale, with no seat counts or web traffic caps, for individuals and teams under 50 employees.

name: Manna
classification_notes: Described as clean and timeless, Manna sits in the geometric sans tradition with a restrained, unfussy character suited to everyday commercial use.
summary: A single-weight geometric sans with a restrained, workaday character aimed at branding and everyday commercial use.
description: Offered as a single Regular weight, Manna covers basic Latin with a limited set of accented characters across 150 glyphs. The design prioritises inoffensive clarity over personality, making it a workable choice for logos, packaging and web use where neutrality is the brief. At $25 with a broad flat-rate commercial licence, it is positioned as a low-friction option for independent designers and small studios.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describe Manna as design's answer to a button-down shirt: clean, timeless, and the safest bet you'll make all day. It comes as a single Regular weight with OTF and webfont files, covered by a flat commercial licence with no seat counts or web traffic caps, suitable for individuals and teams under 50 employees.

name: Misprint
classification_notes: A single-weight stamped-style display face with intentionally rough, uneven letterforms simulating worn or over-printed production output, placing it firmly in the distressed display category.
summary: A single-weight stamped display face with deliberately rough edges and uneven forms that evoke overworked print production.
description: The letterforms carry the texture of repeated passes through a press, with irregular edges and subtle inconsistencies built into each glyph. Supplied as a single Regular weight in OTF and webfont formats, it covers 206 glyphs with broad Western European language support including Icelandic and Welsh. Best suited to logotypes, packaging and short headline work where a worn, tactile quality is the point.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Misprint as a stamped-style display font that looks like it has been through several production runs, with rough edges and uneven forms that give layouts an effortless, characterful quality. A flat commercial licence covers individuals and teams under 50 employees across logos, packaging, websites, apps and broadcast.

name: Napili
classification_notes: A single-weight display face with a relaxed, tropical character described by the foundry as clean and breezy with polish, placing it firmly in casual display territory rather than a functional text category.
summary: A single-weight casual display face with a relaxed, breezy character suited to branding, packaging and editorial headlines.
description: Available in one weight, Napili sits in the space between polished and easygoing, carrying enough personality for display use without tipping into kitsch. The 151-glyph set covers basic Latin with a limited range of accented characters, making it suitable for Western European languages. Reviewers have used it for book covers and client branding work, suggesting it holds up well at larger sizes.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describe Napili as having just enough tropical charm to make a layout feel relaxed without going too far, characterising it as clean and breezy with a bit of polish. It is sold as a single commercial licence covering logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale, with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Noodle
classification_notes: Described as 8-bit in character but deliberately avoiding a strict grid, Noodle sits in the display category as a pixel-inspired face with organic, hand-drawn looseness rather than mechanical rigidity.
summary: A pixel-inflected display face built on the DNA of Milkbar, trading the strict grid of 8-bit type for a looser, more organic rhythm.
description: Where most pixel-style faces are locked to a mechanical grid, Noodle relaxes the construction into something warmer and less precise, described by its maker as what 8-bit looks like when you've only heard rumours about it. It ships as a single Regular weight with 220 glyphs covering Western European languages including Icelandic and Welsh. Letters are drawn to sit snugly at default tracking but hold up equally well with wider spacing.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Noodle as built from the DNA of their earlier typeface Milkbar, ditching the pixel grid in favour of something more human than mechanical. Letters are designed to connect naturally at default tracking, but the designer notes it works just as well with wider spacing, leaving the choice of feel to the user.

name: Quick Draw
classification_notes: A bold, single-weight display face with a rough, hand-drawn character intended for headlines and graphic use; fits squarely in the display category with an expressive, informal subcategory.
summary: A single-weight bold display face with a rough, hand-drawn character aimed at headlines, posters and graphic work.
description: Built for impact at large sizes, it carries a deliberately rough texture that suggests speed and informality. With 166 glyphs and coverage across Western European languages including Icelandic and Welsh, it is broadly serviceable for multilingual display use. At £25 with a flat commercial licence covering most small-business use cases, it sits at the accessible end of the display font market.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Quick Draw as bold, rough and fast on the draw, designed for headlines, graphics, or anything that needs to look like it got there first. It comes as a single Regular weight with OTF and webfont files, covered by a flat commercial licence for individuals and teams under 50 employees with no seat or traffic restrictions.

name: Red Dye 40
classification_notes: A thick, imperfect display face with deliberate irregularity and strong presence, suited to poster and headline use rather than text setting.
summary: A single-weight display face built for punchy, high-confidence headline work with deliberately imperfect, heavy letterforms.
description: Thick strokes and intentional roughness give this display face a tactile, over-confident quality that suits poster work, packaging and logotypes. It ships as a single Regular weight with 206 glyphs covering extensive Western European Latin including Icelandic and Welsh. A flat-rate commercial licence with no seat limits or traffic caps makes it approachable for small studio use.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Red Dye 40 as thick, imperfect and too confident for its own good, available as a single commercial licence covering logos, packaging, websites, apps, broadcast and products for individuals and teams under 50 employees.

name: Road Runner
classification_notes: Described as a refined sans with a slightly retro edge, placing it in the grotesque tradition with subtle vintage character rather than strict geometric or humanist construction.
summary: A single-weight refined sans with a mild retro character, suited to branding and logo work where clarity and a touch of vintage warmth are needed.
description: Clean construction and a slightly retro edge give Road Runner a quiet personality that holds up well in logos and packaging without demanding attention. It ships as a single Regular weight with broad Western European language coverage including Icelandic and Welsh, added in a late 2028 update. The glyph set is lean at 128 characters, which keeps the offering focused rather than expansive.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Road Runner as a refined sans with a slightly retro edge — clean, dependable, and effective even when the colour palette isn't cooperating. It remains one of the most popular fonts in the shop, and a late 2028 update expanded its language support and punctuation to cover all Western European languages including Icelandic and Welsh.

name: Juntos
classification_notes: A bold, quirky hand-drawn display typeface with a rough texture variant and a clean version, designed for decorative and branding use rather than extended text setting.
summary: A bold, textured hand-drawn display typeface made in collaboration with a salon, available in rough and clean cuts.
description: Created in collaboration between Taylor Penton and Abraham of Sabio Salon, Juntos comes in two OTF files: a rough version with hand-drawn texture and a clean version for smoother applications. The character set covers 161 glyphs including basic Latin and a limited range of accented characters. Priced at $25 with a broad single commercial licence covering logos, packaging and web use.
foundry_blurb: Juntos is described by Taylor Penton as bold, a little quirky, and deliberately free of fancy ligatures, designed to bring a personal, handcrafted quality to branding and design projects. It was made in collaboration with Abraham at Sabio Salon and ships as two OTF files covering the rough and clean variants.

name: Kari
designer: Taylor Penton, Abraham of Sabio Salon
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a display font by the foundry, with regular and condensed widths toggled via the shift key rather than separate font files, placing it squarely in the display category.
summary: A collaborative display font with regular and condensed widths baked into a single OTF, toggled via the shift key.
description: Designed by Taylor Penton in collaboration with Abraham of Sabio Salon, Kari packages two widths into one file, with the condensed variant accessed by holding shift rather than loading a separate font. The glyph set covers 151 characters with basic Latin and a limited selection of accented forms. Suited to logos, packaging and short display settings where a hand-drawn quality is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describe Kari as the result of two designers committing to a decent idea together, made in collaboration with Abraham of Sabio Salon. It ships as a single OTF with regular and condensed widths accessed via the shift key, which they cheekily note could be called a variable font but that would be generous.

name: The People's Bundle
classification_notes: A collection of six distinct display typefaces spanning hand-drawn, decorative and expressive styles, best grouped under display as none are text or body faces.
summary: A bundle of six display typefaces from Taylor Penton, each with a distinct hand-drawn or expressive character suited to branding and visual identity work.
description: Six fonts sold together at a flat price: Taxi Driver, Misprint, Birdie, Road Runner, Yamas! and Milkbar. Each covers uppercase letters, digits, full punctuation and over 50 languages, delivered in OTF and WOFF/WOFF2 formats. The range sits comfortably in logo, packaging and social media work where personality and legibility at large sizes matter more than typographic finesse.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes the bundle as a collection of his most popular fonts, born out of what he calls his extremely hot office. The single commercial licence covers individuals, small businesses and teams under 50 employees, with no seat counts or web traffic caps, and extends to logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale.

name: Roony
classification_notes: Described explicitly as hand-drawn lettering with a casual, organic character; its single-weight, informal construction places it firmly in the display category rather than any structured typographic tradition.
summary: A single-weight hand-drawn display face with a loose, organic quality suited to casual branding and packaging.
description: Roony is a hand-lettered display typeface with an unpretentious, sketched character that reviewers have used for category headers, branding proposals and vintage-leaning work. It ships as a single Regular weight with basic Latin coverage and a limited set of accented characters across 150 glyphs. The lack of weights or italics keeps it a narrow-use tool, but within that scope it does what it sets out to do.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton markets Roony as a free-range, ethically sourced font with no additives, preservatives or weird aftertaste, promising honest hand-drawn letters that pair well with anything. A single commercial licence covers individuals, small businesses and teams under 50 employees across logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale.

name: Rosemary
classification_notes: The page describes restrained, carefully drawn strokes with a quiet personality, consistent with a humanist sans-serif approach that prioritises legibility and subtle character over geometric rigidity.
summary: A single-weight humanist sans with restrained personality, aimed at branding, packaging and everyday commercial use.
description: Available only in Regular, Rosemary is a quietly characterful sans-serif with careful stroke work that sits comfortably across logos, packaging and web use. The glyph set covers 151 characters with basic Latin and a modest selection of accented forms for Western European languages. Its accessible, even texture and clean construction make it a reliable workhorse at modest price.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describe Rosemary as the white T-shirt of fonts, crafted with careful strokes that make a statement without yelling. It ships as a single Regular weight in OTF and webfont formats under a flat commercial licence covering individuals, small businesses and teams under 50 employees, with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Amaya
classification_notes: Tall proportions with occasional wide letters and a distinctive ampersand suggest an expressive display serif designed for headlines and identity work rather than text setting.
summary: A single-weight display serif with tall proportions and irregular width rhythms, aimed at logos, packaging and headline use.
description: The typeface pairs upright, elongated letterforms with occasional wide characters that introduce an uneven but deliberate cadence. At 150 glyphs it covers basic Latin with a limited accented set, making it a focused tool for display applications rather than extended text. The notably characterful ampersand is flagged by the designer as a draw in its own right.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Amaya as walking the line between survival gear and elegant display, with tall proportions and occasional wide letters giving it a steady yet human rhythm. They call it a little weird and a little beautiful, and single out the ampersand as worth the download alone. It ships as a single Regular weight with OTF desktop and WOFF/WOFF2 webfont files under a flat commercial licence covering individuals and teams under 50 employees.

name: Rug Pull
classification_notes: Described as stamped and hand-drawn with intentional irregularity in rhythm and spacing, placing it firmly in the hand-lettered display category rather than any formal type classification.
summary: A single-weight hand-drawn display face with deliberately irregular rhythm and worn, stamped character.
description: Built from hand-drawn lettering with a stamped, imperfect quality, it makes no attempt to disguise its rough edges or uneven spacing. That intentional irregularity gives it a tactile, analogue feel suited to branding, packaging and editorial headlines where a mechanical typeface would feel too polished. Covers Western European Latin including Icelandic and Welsh across 207 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describe Rug Pull as a typeface that doesn't follow the rules, hand-drawn and stamped with off-rhythm spacing and kerning that has a mind of its own, yet somehow still works. It comes with a straightforward commercial licence covering logos, packaging, websites, apps and broadcast with no seat or traffic limits for individuals and teams under 50 employees.

name: Bang!
classification_notes: A single-weight display face inspired by cross-stitch and embroidery aesthetics, with an intentionally irregular, stitched quality rather than a clean geometric or calligraphic construction.
summary: A single-weight display face drawn from cross-stitch and embroidery references, with a deliberately loose, stitched character.
description: Built from a late-night detour through cross-stitch imagery, the face trades the rigid grid typical of needlework lettering for something more relaxed and irregular. It ships as a single Regular weight covering 220 glyphs with broad Western European language support including Icelandic and Welsh. Best suited to casual branding, packaging and social graphics where a handmade, textile-adjacent quality is the point.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Bang! as the product of falling down a cross-stitch rabbit hole on Pinterest and deciding to make a font that wasn't so rigid. It comes as a single OTF with web font files, priced at $25 under a flat commercial licence covering individuals and teams of up to 50 employees.

name: Barrera
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a display font with natural irregularities from hand-drawing; its quiet, clean character places it in the hand-drawn display subcategory rather than script or brush.
summary: A single-weight hand-drawn display face with clean shapes and deliberate irregularities, designed in Maui.
description: Drawn by hand in Maui, it carries the subtle inconsistencies of a natural drawing process without tipping into rough or overly casual territory. The 150-glyph set covers basic Latin with a limited range of accented characters, making it suitable for logos, packaging and short display settings. A modest single-weight offering that sits comfortably in branding work where a restrained personality is preferable to high drama.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Barrera as a display font with quiet presence, hand-drawn in Maui. It offers clean shapes, natural irregularities and a balance suited to projects that need a little personality without a lot of drama. A single commercial licence covers individuals, small businesses and teams under 50 employees across logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale.

name: Benditos
classification_notes: Inspired by vintage hotel matchbooks, Benditos is a display typeface with a retro character that places it firmly in the display category, drawing on mid-century ephemera rather than any formal typographic tradition.
summary: A single-weight display face drawn from vintage hotel matchbook lettering, with enough structure to sit comfortably in contemporary branding work.
description: Drawn from the aesthetic of vintage hotel matchbooks, Benditos brings a mid-century retro quality to display and branding contexts without feeling purely nostalgic. It ships as a single Regular weight in OTF, WOFF and WOFF2 formats, with basic Latin coverage and a limited set of accented characters. Suited to logos, packaging and campaign work where a warm, slightly irreverent personality is appropriate.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describe Benditos as one of the few pre-blessed fonts you can legally download, inspired by vintage hotel matchbooks and balancing old-school charm with a structure that holds up today. They pitch it as a little retro, a little modern, and suited to projects that could use a little divine intervention.

name: Barro
classification_notes: Barro is a casual handwriting script with rounded strokes and deliberate imperfection, placing it firmly in the informal handwritten script subcategory rather than formal calligraphic script.
summary: A casual handwriting script with rounded strokes and controlled irregularity, pitched at branding and packaging work that wants a human touch without looking homemade.
description: Rounded letterforms and subtle movement give it warmth without sacrificing legibility, sitting somewhere between a polished logotype script and a personal note. A single Regular weight covers the full use case, supplied as OTF, WOFF and WOFF2 with basic Latin and a limited set of accented characters across 150 glyphs. Suited to festival branding, product packaging and promotional materials where personality matters more than typographic range.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Barro as handwriting with its act together: rounded strokes, subtle movement, and just enough imperfection to stay interesting. It lands between casual and considered, making it ideal for projects that want to feel human rather than homemade. The licence covers logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale under a single flat fee for individuals, small businesses and teams under 50 employees.

name: Bartender
classification_notes: A vintage-styled display serif with strong curves and deliberate texture, suited to packaging and signage rather than text setting. The foundry's own framing points to a nostalgic, decorative character consistent with display classification.
summary: A single-weight display serif with vintage texture and strong curves, aimed at packaging, signage and logo work with a nostalgic character.
description: Coming in one Regular weight, it offers 166 glyphs covering Western European languages including Icelandic and Welsh. The design leans on deliberate imperfection and period-influenced curves to suggest age and handcraft. Best suited to short-setting contexts where a worn, vintage tone is the point.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describe Bartender as a serif for people who romanticise decades they never lived through, with vintage bones, strong curves and enough texture to feel human. They recommend it for packaging, signage and anything that benefits from looking older than its maker.

name: Tommy Tokyo
classification_notes: A hybrid collegiate-style display face with softened curves, placing it squarely in the display category with a sporty, varsity-inspired subcategory.
summary: A collegiate display face with softened curves, designed to sit somewhere between varsity lettering and a more relaxed, approachable style.
description: Tommy Tokyo takes the structural language of collegiate and varsity type and rounds off the harder edges, giving it a looser, more contemporary character. It ships as a single Regular weight with basic Latin coverage and a limited set of accented characters, totalling 151 glyphs. Best suited to logos, packaging and apparel where a sporty but unforced tone is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Tommy Tokyo as a hybrid collegiate-style display font with added curves to soften the genre's usual rigidity. The foundry pitches it with self-deprecating humour, selling it as a single commercial licence with no seat counts or web traffic caps, covering logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale.

name: Basal
classification_notes: Revived from a circa 1910 bottle label, the typeface carries the sturdy, stripped-back character of early twentieth-century commercial lettering, placing it firmly in the display and vintage revival category.
summary: A single-weight revival drawn from a circa 1910 bottle label, stripped back to its most functional form for branding and packaging work.
description: Pulled from early twentieth-century bottle lettering and updated for contemporary use, this is a compact, no-frills display face with a distinctly utilitarian character. It ships as a single Regular weight covering basic Latin with a limited selection of accented characters, making it best suited to short-form applications such as logos, labels and packaging. The glyph set of 150 characters is lean, so it works hardest at headline sizes where its period source material shows most clearly.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Basal as derived from a bottle found circa 1910 and updated just enough for modern use, noting its sturdy, stripped-back quality and suitability for building projects from the ground up. It comes with a flat-rate commercial licence covering individuals, small businesses and teams under 50 employees, with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Waffle Cone
classification_notes: A thick, wide display typeface with a single weight, designed explicitly for maximum visual impact at large sizes rather than text use. Its bold, informal character places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-weight, heavy display typeface built for headlines and packaging where a thick, wide presence is the entire point.
description: Wide proportions and heavy strokes make it unsuitable for text but well-suited to logos, packaging, t-shirt graphics and poster headlines. The glyph set covers 151 characters with basic Latin and a modest selection of accented letters. Sold as a flat-rate commercial licence with no seat or traffic restrictions, which makes it accessible for small studios and independent designers.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describe Waffle Cone as a typeface for when subtle just isn't it — thick, wide and impossible to ignore. It was made to do the heavy lifting in design contexts that call for bold, attention-grabbing type rather than restraint.

name: Bea
classification_notes: The foundry's description of 'strong personality, soft edges, and full of character' alongside customer references to a linocut quality suggest a display-oriented serif with textured, expressive letterforms rather than a text-setting workhorse.
summary: A single-weight display serif with a linocut quality, designed as a personal tribute and suited to packaging, branding and invitation work.
description: Available in a single Regular weight, Bea is a display serif with soft edges and a handcrafted character that customers describe as having a linocut quality. The glyph set covers 151 characters including basic Latin and a limited range of accented forms. At £25 with a straightforward commercial licence covering logos, packaging and web use, it occupies a clear niche in the small-business branding market.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Bea as inspired by his wife, sharing her bold and beautiful qualities and a capacity to brighten up just about anything. The typeface is said to have a strong personality with soft edges and full character, intended for use when something personal and distinctive is called for.

name: Walkout
classification_notes: Walkout is a hand-drawn display typeface with intentionally uneven letterforms and protest-influenced aesthetics, placing it firmly in the expressive display category rather than any regularised script or sans tradition.
summary: A hand-drawn display face with deliberately uneven letterforms, drawn from protest sign aesthetics and aimed at headlines and disruptive typographic work.
description: Strong, irregular strokes give this face a raw, handmade quality that sits apart from polished brush scripts or clean display types. It comes as a single Regular weight covering 206 glyphs with broad Western European language support, including Icelandic and Welsh. Best used large, in short bursts, where its deliberate unevenness reads as energy rather than imprecision.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Walkout as a hand-drawn display typeface with protest roots, strong and a little uneven, built for headlines, statements and disruptive typography. It includes a flat-rate commercial licence for individuals, small businesses and teams under 50 employees, covering logos, packaging, websites, apps, broadcast and products for sale.

name: Watkins
classification_notes: The page describes Watkins as rooted in traditional forms with a softness that reads as modern, pointing to a transitional or contemporary serif with humanist warmth rather than strict oldstyle or neoclassical geometry.
summary: A single-weight serif with traditional proportions and a softened texture suited to branding and packaging work.
description: Watkins ships as a single Regular weight with 150 glyphs covering basic Latin and a limited set of accented characters. The design sits between classical serif conventions and a lighter, more approachable register, making it practical for logos, packaging and short-form display text. Coverage is modest and there is no italic or additional weight, so it works best as a display or headline face rather than extended body copy.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describe Watkins as a typeface that plays nice with tradition but still does its own thing — bold, clean and rooted in traditional forms, with enough softness to feel modern. It comes with a straightforward flat-fee commercial licence covering individuals and teams under 50 employees, with no seat counts or web traffic caps, in OTF and webfont formats.

name: Sweetheart
classification_notes: A loosely drawn handwritten italic script imitating casual pen lettering, with natural irregularity and an informal, personal quality rather than formal calligraphic structure.
summary: A loosely drawn handwritten italic that mimics the uneven, sincere quality of pen lettering found in personal correspondence.
description: Drawn from the aesthetic of handwritten love notes, this script leans into natural irregularity rather than the polished consistency of formal brush scripts. A single Regular weight is supplied as OTF, WOFF and WOFF2, covering basic Latin with a limited set of accented characters across 150 glyphs. Best suited to packaging, branding and editorial contexts where casual warmth is more useful than technical precision.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Sweetheart as a handwritten italic pulled from the margins of an old love note — loosely drawn, a little uneven, and more sincere than polished. They position it as ideal for when you want something that feels personal but not overly precious, sold under a single flat commercial licence with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Brunch
classification_notes: Described by the designer as part geometric, part handmade, placing it in the display category with a hybrid subcategory that blends constructed letterforms with informal, hand-drawn qualities.
summary: A single-weight display face that sits between geometric construction and handmade informality, aimed at branding and packaging.
description: Sold as a single Regular weight with 151 glyphs, Brunch covers basic Latin with a modest set of accented characters for Western European languages. The design blends geometric structure with hand-drawn character, making it a natural fit for consumer branding, food packaging and social media graphics. Licensing is straightforward: one flat fee covers commercial use for individuals and small teams without seat or traffic restrictions.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Brunch as sitting somewhere in between: not too much, not too little, with a balance that is part geometric and part handmade. The single commercial licence covers logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale, with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Tall Boy
classification_notes: Described as inspired by Road Runner and positioned as a tall, narrow counterpart to wide display typefaces, placing it firmly in the condensed display category suited to headlines and branding.
summary: A single-weight condensed display face inspired by Road Runner, designed for headlines and branding work that needs a tall, narrow presence.
description: Built as a deliberate counterpoint to wide, heavy display typefaces, Tall Boy draws inspiration from Road Runner to deliver a tall, slender silhouette suited to posters, logos and packaging. It ships as a single Regular weight with basic Latin coverage and a limited set of accented characters across 151 glyphs. Licensing is flat-rate with no seat counts or traffic caps, covering commercial use for individuals and teams up to 50 employees.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton positions Tall Boy as a corrective to recent wide, heavy display releases, describing it as a tall glass of water inspired by Road Runner. Sold as a single commercial licence with no seats or web traffic limits, covering logos, packaging, websites, client work, apps, broadcast and products for sale.

name: Stupid Cupid
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface with an organic, hand-drawn character, placing it in the display mono subcategory rather than a utilitarian coding font.
summary: A single-weight monospaced display font with an organic, slightly erratic character, positioned as a companion to the designer's Cupid typeface.
description: Built on a fixed-width grid but drawn with an informal, welcoming hand, it sits somewhere between a display novelty and a usable branding tool. The glyph set covers Basic Latin with a limited selection of accented characters, totalling 150 glyphs. Suited to short-form work where a relaxed, lo-fi mono aesthetic is wanted without the sterility of a coding font.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Stupid Cupid as monospaced and mildly unhinged, calling it the not-so-distant cousin to their Cupid typeface. They position it as welcoming and organic, filling a niche space in the fontbook for those who want a monospaced option with personality.

name: Wawa
classification_notes: A wide, relaxed display typeface designed for short, bold headlines and titles, with a tropical, leisurely character that places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A wide, unhurried display face with a tropical sensibility, suited to short headlines, packaging and logo work.
description: Built for bold, short phrases and eye-catching titles, Wawa takes a deliberately broad and relaxed stance that suits it to lifestyle branding, packaging and editorial headlines. The single Regular weight ships as OTF, WOFF and WOFF2, covering basic Latin with a limited set of accented characters across 151 glyphs. Its character sits somewhere between a beach bar sign and a confident logotype, which gives it more range than its narrow weight offering might suggest.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Wawa as a tropical-inspired display font that knows how to take its time, calling it wide, relaxed, and happiest when used for bold, short phrases or eye-catching titles. A single commercial licence covers individuals, small businesses and teams under 50 employees, with no seat counts or web traffic caps.

name: Big Bird
classification_notes: A vintage-style stamp typeface with deliberate hand-drawn inconsistencies, placing it firmly in the display category as a decorative, textural face rather than a functional text typeface.
summary: A single-weight vintage stamp typeface with intentional hand-drawn irregularities, evolved from the designer's earlier Birdie font.
description: Drawing on his earlier Birdie typeface but executed with a lighter touch, Taylor Penton has built a stamp-style display face loaded with subtle hand-drawn inconsistencies. The 151-glyph set covers Western European languages including Icelandic and Welsh, and ships as OTF with webfont formats included. Well suited to packaging, logos and branding work that calls for an unpolished, analogue quality.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Big Bird as a vintage-style stamp typeface inspired by one of his first fonts, Birdie, but approached with a lighter hand. It is loaded with hand-drawn traits and subtle inconsistencies, and the foundry notes its applications are broad, saying it will feel right at home regardless of the use case. A November 2025 update expanded language support and punctuation coverage.

name: Yamas!
classification_notes: A single-weight, high-contrast display face with hand-drawn origins and a deliberate wobble; the wavy alternates and informal energy place it firmly in the expressive display category rather than any conventional serif or sans classification.
summary: A hand-drawn, high-contrast display face with a subtle wobble and wavy stylistic alternates accessed via Shift.
description: Drawn in a single sitting with the same song on loop, Yamas! carries visible hand-made energy through high contrast strokes and a gentle irregularity in the letterforms. A set of wavy alternates adds a second register for when a looser, more undulating feel is wanted. The 220-glyph set covers basic Latin plus a broad range of accented characters for Western and Central European languages.
foundry_blurb: Taylor Penton describes Yamas! as a soft, high-contrast typeface with a subtle wobble and a little charm baked in, hand-drawn with the same song on loop for days. Pressing Shift unlocks wavy alternates for when straight lines aren't the vibe. It is one of the most downloaded fonts in the shop and was updated in January 2026 with broader language support and expanded punctuation.
